mnemonics.title: Linked List Parts
mnemonics.content: NODE = DATA + POINTER - Remember: Every node has its data and a 'pointer' to move forward.

mnemonics.title: Linked List Operations
mnemonics.content: INSERT DELETE SEARCH TRAVERSE - ISSET: Insert (beginning/end/middle), Search (linear), Sort (not efficient), Traverse (visit all), Empty check, Delete (beginning/end/middle)

mnemonics.title: Time Complexities
mnemonics.content: HEAD INSERT/DELETE = O(1), TAIL = O(n), SEARCH = O(n) - Remember: HITS - Head Insert/Delete are Same Time

acronyms.expansion: Linked List - A linear data structure with nodes containing data and pointers
acronyms.usage: Used when dynamic size and efficient insertions are needed

acronyms.expansion: Singly Linked List - Each node has only next pointer
acronyms.usage: Used for simple forward traversal applications

acronyms.expansion: Doubly Linked List - Each node has both next and previous pointers
acronyms.usage: Used when traversal in both directions is required

acronyms.expansion: Not a Number or Null Pointer - Indicates end of list in SLL
acronyms.usage: Used to mark the end of the linked list

acronyms.expansion: Head Pointer - Reference to the first node in the list
acronyms.usage: Starting point for all linked list operations

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box split into two parts: LEFT box labeled 'DATA' and RIGHT box labeled 'NEXT' with an arrow pointing to another node. For doubly, add a LEFT pointer arrow pointing backward.
Draw nodes as rectangles in a row but NOT touching each other. Draw arrows from each node's NEXT part pointing to the next rectangle. The last arrow points to NULL (drawn as ground symbol or empty).
Draw new node on left, draw arrow from its NEXT to existing first node, then draw arrow from HEAD to new node. Shows HEAD now points to new node.
Draw HEAD arrow moving from first node to second node. Show first node being 'detached' (perhaps in dashed lines) being removed.
Same as singly but the last node's arrow loops back and points to the first node instead of NULL. No ground symbol at the end.

analogies.analogy: Think of a treasure hunt where each clue (node) tells you where to find the next clue. You start at the first clue (HEAD) and follow directions (NEXT pointers) to find each subsequent treasure.
Like a train where each carriage (node) has passengers (data) and a coupling (pointer) that connects to the next carriage. You can only move forward by following the couplings.
Arrays are like fixed-size train with pre-assigned seats - you must know passenger count beforehand. Linked lists are like a shuttle bus - you can add or remove buses as people arrive.
Walking through a museum where each room (node) has a sign pointing to the next room. You enter (HEAD), read the room, follow the sign to the next, until you reach the exit (NULL).
